title: Problem 1.1
statement_text: Is it true that for every 2-edge-connected graph $G$, the reconfiguration graph $\mathcal{F}(G,5)$ is connected?
context_text: This is the natural reconfiguration version of Tutte's 5-flow conjecture. The paper notes that Problem 1.1 is incomparable to Tutte's 5-flow conjecture itself, since connectedness of $\mathcal{F}(G,k)$ does not imply non-emptiness. The group-flow analogue is posed separately as Problem 1.3.

title: Problem 1.2
statement_text: Is there an integer $k\geqslant 5$, such that for every 2-edge-connected graph $G$, the reconfiguration graph $\mathcal{F}(G,k)$ is connected?
context_text: This is a weaker variant of Problem 1.1: rather than asking whether $k=5$ suffices, it asks whether any fixed integer $k$ makes the integer-flow reconfiguration graph universally connected. It is posed immediately after Problem 1.1 as a relaxation of interest.

title: Problem 1.3
statement_text: Is it true that for every 2-edge-connected graph $G$, the reconfiguration graph $\mathcal{F}(G,\mathbb{Z}_{5})$ is connected?
context_text: This is the group-flow analogue of Problem 1.1, asking about $\mathbb{Z}_5$-flows instead of integer 5-flows. The paper shows that connectivity of the flow reconfiguration graph depends on the group structure, making the group and integer settings genuinely distinct.

title: Problem 1.4
statement_text: Is there an abelian group $A$, such that for every 2-edge-connected graph $G$, the reconfiguration graph $\mathcal{F}(G,A)$ is connected?
context_text: This asks for the existence of any abelian group making the group-flow reconfiguration graph universally connected. The paper shows that frozen configurations (isolated vertices) do not exist in $\mathcal{F}(G,A)$ whenever $|A|=4$ or $|A|\geqslant 6$, but that $\mathcal{F}(G,\mathbb{Z}_5)$ can contain frozen flows, complicating the picture.

title: Problem 2.6
statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph and $k$ be an integer. Prove that if $\mathcal{F}(G,\mathbb{Z}_{k})$ is connected, then $\mathcal{F}(G,k)$ is also connected.
context_text: The paper establishes the easier direction (connectivity of $\mathcal{F}(G,k)$ implies connectivity of $\mathcal{F}(G,\mathbb{Z}_k)$) and provides examples showing the group structure matters. The converse direction is open; the authors note they cannot prove it even for $k=4$.
